event_blocks.summary: A woman, Holly from Hot Humble Pie, wearing a black hat, greets viewers with upbeat background music before transitioning to a black-and-white shot where she, in a cowboy hat, encourages subscriptions while holding a glue gun. The focus then shifts to a top-down view of a light-colored woven basket on a white surface, introduced as a Dollar Tree item for a DIY project. Holly's hands, with rings and red nail polish, use a red pencil and metal ruler to mark the basket for a high-end design, explaining that imperfections are acceptable due to the material and upcoming paint. She then uses a black 'Painters pen' to draw bold lines along the marks, defining the geometric shapes. Transitioning to transparent gloves, her hands apply a watered-down dark brown paint within these marked sections using a paintbrush, noting the color is lighter than preferred and that geometric designs prevent her usual spray technique. The basket is rotated for complete coverage, including the interior, before a smaller brush adds black paint to the basket's base center, creating a sharp accent. A container of dark paint and its lid are visible, underscoring the methodical and precise transformation of the basket.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a woman, identified by her voice as Holly from Hot Humble Pie, wearing a black hat and smiling at the camera, as she greets viewers with upbeat background music. A quick transition shows a hand placing a potted plant with pink flowers on a table outdoors, while text overlays announce '20 Dollar Tree DIY Planter Hacks'. The scene shifts to a black-and-white shot of Holly, now wearing a cowboy hat and holding a glue gun, with a 'SUBSCRIBE' button visible, as she encourages viewers to subscribe to her channel. The video then transitions to a top-down view of a light-colored woven basket, which Holly introduces as a 'Dollar Tree basket' and the main item for her DIY, placed on a clean white surface. Her hands, adorned with rings and red nail polish, use a red pencil and a metal ruler to make initial marks on the basket’s side, preparing to 'dress it up' with a shape she describes as looking 'really expensive and high-end.' Holly explains that the lines don't need to be perfect because the basket will be painted and its woven, uneven texture makes precise freehanding challenging. Her hands then switch to a black marker, which she refers to as her 'Painters pen in black,' to draw bold, straight lines along the marked sections, tracing the shape as a boundary.

The video opens with a female speaker's voice explaining her crafting process. Hands, adorned with rings and red nail polish, use a Black Marker labeled 'Painters' to draw bold, angular 'geometrical designs' on a light-colored, rectangular Woven Basket placed on a clean White Surface. The speaker clarifies that she uses a 'paint pen' for the edges, noting that one could probably use a 'permanent marker as well in black,' but she finds the paint pen a 'safer route.' After marking, the Hands, now wearing transparent gloves, switch to a paintbrush and apply a dark brown stain within the marked sections, carefully following the lines. The speaker describes this as 'water-down brown paint' and mentions that the resulting 'light stained color' is 'a little bit too light for me,' adding that this is 'personal preference.' She explains that because she's doing 'geometrical designs,' she can't water down the paint and spray the basket, which is what she would normally do. As the staining progresses, the basket is rotated to access all sides, and the Hands continue to fill in the marked regions, including the inside, which the speaker notes is important because 'you're going to see that.' Toward the end, the Hands use a smaller brush to apply black paint into the center of the basket’s base, where the lines converge, adding a sharp, defined accent. A small container of dark paint and its lid are visible on the White Surface, indicating the materials used. The process is methodical and precise, transforming the basket’s appearance through deliberate marking and staining, with the speaker providing continuous commentary on her techniques and preferences.

event_blocks.summary: Hands, wearing transparent gloves, apply a dark brown stain to marked sections of the Woven Basket with a paintbrush, tapping the brush to ensure full coverage on the wicker, especially along the 3D edges, resulting in a great finish. Black paint is then applied to the center of the basket's base, accentuating its geometric design. Subsequently, a wooden dowel, identified as a cut and stained black Dollar Tree plunger, is sanded with a white sanding block. This dowel, fashioned into four legs, is attached to the basket's base using a hot glue gun, with the voiceover explaining that only hot glue is sufficient for a secure attachment once dry, eliminating the need for screws. The basket is rotated to showcase its newly attached, sturdy legs. The finished Woven Basket, now elevated on its black legs and holding lush green foliage, is displayed on a white surface, described as a cute, high-end planter upgrade. The video ends with an overlay promoting a 'Midcentury Bamboo Stoneware Lidded Vase Collection' accompanied by upbeat, jazzy background music.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a product showcase of a 'Midcentury Bamboo Stoneware Lidded Vase Collection,' followed by a female speaker's introduction to recreating a popular "ribbed look" with visible seams. Transitioning to a top-down view on a white surface, hands with rings and red-painted nails assemble composite objects using the tops of Dollar Tree plungers (terracotta ceramic pieces) and chicken breast cans (metal canisters). Adhesive from a tube is applied to a metal canister's rim before a terracotta lid is attached, a process repeated for multiple units. The speaker suggests alternative materials like the inside of masking tape rolls or small round containers, emphasizing creative freedom. Still images display crafting supplies including metal canisters, beige masking tape, and red metallic cylindrical tubes. The hands continue assembling terracotta and metal components, demonstrating the tutorial's focus on intentionally exposed seams. The first step involves gluing two cans together. A floral arrangement with green leaves and purple/blue flowers adorns the workspace. Subsequently, the crafter's hands apply B-Fix all adhesive from an adhesive tube to a metal canister, then glue a terracotta ceramic piece (plunger) into the open part of the can, assembling one can with a plunger and two cans stacked with a plunger, noting that hot glue is unsuitable for this step, recommending E6000 or Super Glue and advising to let it dry. Despite this, a hot glue gun is then used to attach a red metallic cylindrical tube to the assembly, followed by wrapping it with twine from Walmart. A pink paintbrush seals the twine, and a purple lighter is used to burn off stray fibers for a neat finish. The scene shifts to gloved hands painting a black-painted object white with a brush on a tray lined with aluminum foil, set against a white surface with a floral arrangement, while the narrator expresses dissatisfaction with a previous black spray paint attempt, deeming it "terrible."

event_blocks.summary: Hands, wearing transparent gloves, meticulously apply white paint to a black, bowl-shaped object using a Paintbrush, with the object resting on an Aluminum Foil-lined Tray and a Paint Container nearby. A narrator explains her creative process, noting the previous black paint was flat and she disliked the 'furry look' of the twine, deciding to use leftover paint to create a 'faux concrete' texture, confident it would be 'perfect' to add 'just enough texture,' 'hide the twine,' and result in a 'really high-end, modern-looking planter.' She then reveals the planters are 'made completely from toilet plungers,' exclaiming, 'Look at that! It's beautiful.' After painting, the object is set aside, transitioning to display two Finished Planters, white and textured with a ribbed design, dried to a matte finish and filled with lush green artificial foliage, placed on a White Surface. Uplifting instrumental background music begins as a decorative Text Overlay appears, reading, 'Add a small lift to the center for this beautiful look!', offering a design tip for the elegant display.

event_blocks.summary: A clear glass bowl, initially appearing as 'faux crystal' yet 'plastic look' with an intricate embossed floral pattern, is transformed into a high-end planter. Hands wearing transparent gloves meticulously apply thick white spackling to the interior of the bowl using a paintbrush, covering the detailed patterns 'like frosting on a cake' to preserve the exterior design. A small wooden dowel, referred to as a 'wine cork,' is used to apply the spackling in circular motions, imitating hammered copper and creating a textured finish. After coating the bowl's interior and wiping the rim, it is flipped upside down. A flat, black circular object, identified as a protein drink lid, is attached to the bowl's base using 'Fix-All ADHESIVE' from a white and yellow tube, as hot glue is deemed insufficient for plastic-on-plastic adhesion. The speaker notes that any lid or object can be used for elevation, with the option of adding legs for a personalized look. The assembled item is then spray-painted outdoors with Rust-Oleum Hammered spray paint in a bronze finish, resulting in a 'gorgeous,' on-trend, textured decorative item. The completed bowl, now a rustic planter filled with lush green artificial succulents, is displayed on a clean white surface, showcasing its transformed, metallic-accentuated floral embossing.

raw_segments: The segment opens with an upbeat, light instrumental track as a static shot displays a finished white planter with lush green artificial foliage, placed on a clean white surface. The scene then transitions to a top-down view where a female voice begins to explain her crafting process, stating she saw the intricately patterned clear glass bowl at the Dollar Tree and loved its outside texture, intending to transform it into a high-end planter despite its original 'faux crystal' yet 'plastic look.' She explains her decision to hand-paint rather than spray-paint to avoid a cheap finish, as hands wearing transparent gloves meticulously apply thick white spackling from a container to the interior of the bowl using a paintbrush. The spackling is applied in smooth, circular motions, covering the detailed embossed patterns 'like frosting on a cake,' with the voice noting it's 'really, really easy.' The video opens with a top-down view of a crafting process on a clean white surface, as a female voice explains this is a 'Pinterest-inspired craft' originally from HomeTalk. A pair of hands, one gloved and the other bare with red nails and rings, uses a small wooden dowel—which the speaker refers to as a 'wine cork'—to apply a thick white substance, identified as 'spackling,' to the interior of a clear glass bowl. The hands spread the substance in circular motions, creating an embossed, swirling pattern that the speaker notes is meant to 'imitate hammered copper,' adding that she 'absolutely loves the texture' it creates. After fully coating the bowl, the hands wipe the rim and flip it upside down. The speaker then introduces the next step, stating she's using 'that little Fix-All again' because they're 'dealing with plastic on plastic' and 'hot glue just isn't going to cut it.' A flat, black circular object, which the speaker reveals is 'a lid to a protein drink that my son was drinking,' is placed on the bowl’s bottom. Adhesive from a white and yellow tube labeled 'Fix-All ADHESIVE' is applied around its edge, and the hands firmly press the black lid onto the bowl’s base, securing it in place to create a textured decorative item.

The video opens with a close-up of hands, adorned with rings and red-painted nails, applying black paint to the base of an inverted white, textured glass bowl with an embossed floral pattern using a wooden dowel, spreading it in circular motions. A female voice explains, "you can use any lid that you can find or anything that you want to rise this up with, you could even put little legs on it if you would prefer. The female voice continues, "Then I took it outside and gave it a coat of this hammered spray paint and this is what we ended up with. Look at that, you guys. I hope the camera does it justice because it came up absolutely gorgeous." The bowl, now fully coated in the hammered bronze paint, is rotated to display its transformed, textured surface, with a zoom-in highlighting the intricate floral embossing accentuated by the metallic finish. The final frames reveal the completed bowl, now serving as an elegant, rustic planter filled with lush green artificial succulents, placed on a clean white surface. From 0:22, upbeat, rhythmic electronic music with synth melodies begins, continuing with increasing intensity and percussive elements until the end of the minute.

event_blocks.summary: A pair of hands with red-painted nails and rings meticulously wraps a beige, ribbed plastic bowl with natural-colored twine, identified as sisal rope, using a white and gray hot glue gun to secure the material in dabs. The speaker explains the motivation for the project, expressing a desire for a wrapped planter, and demonstrates how to cut the twine at an angle with black-handled scissors to create an inconspicuous end, subsequently burning the cut ends with a purple lighter to prevent fraying. The speaker notes the natural qualities of the sisal rope, including its texture and scent reminiscent of hay. The hands then apply black paint to the bowl's base with a small paintbrush for a geometric accent. A transparent stencil, identified as a Dollar General stencil featuring a leaf pattern, is placed on the bowl, and the hands carefully paint over it with black paint using a brush, avoiding sponging as it proved ineffective, to reveal a crisp, decorative leaf design on the textured planter against a white surface.

raw_segments: The video opens by showcasing a finished bronze-painted planter filled with lush green artificial succulents, representing the completed DIY project. As upbeat, slightly electronic background music begins to play, the scene transitions to a crafting workspace. A female voice explains, "For this DIY, I took one of these Dollar Tree planters here... and I just started to put hot glue around the bottom and use sisal rope to wrap this up." A pair of hands, adorned with red-painted nails and rings, are seen wrapping a beige, ribbed plastic bowl—identified by the speaker as a Dollar Tree planter—with natural-colored twine, which the speaker clarifies is sisal rope. The hands methodically apply hot glue in small dabs along the bowl’s exterior using a white and gray hot glue gun, then firmly press the sisal rope into place, rotating the bowl to ensure tight, continuous, and even coverage. The speaker further elaborates on her motivation, stating, "Believe it or not, I do not have a wrapped planter anywhere in my home. I've wrapped a couple things with twine and with sisal rope, but I have yet to do this and I want one. I think they're so, so cute. So, I'm just gonna go wrap it up." The segment opens with calm acoustic guitar music as a pair of hands, adorned with red-painted nails and rings, meticulously wraps a ribbed beige bowl with natural beige twine, securing it with dabs of hot glue from a white and gray glue gun. The female speaker explains her technique, stating, "at the end here, I'm just going to go ahead and cut it at an angle," using black-handled scissors to trim the excess twine. She elaborates that this "great little trick" prevents an "obvious finished end" where the twine begins and ends. After neatly burning the cut ends with a purple lighter to prevent fraying, the speaker identifies the material as "sisal rope," noting its "little grass pieces" and how it "actually smells like hay," emphasizing its natural product quality that she loves in her home. The hands then apply black paint to the bowl's base with a small paintbrush, creating a bold geometric accent. Finally, a transparent stencil with leaf and swirl patterns is placed on the bowl, and the speaker reveals, "now I'm using a Dollar General stencil," specifically using the "little leaf." She advises against sponging, stating, "I will tell you I tried sponging it first and it did not work. You do need a brush," as the hands carefully paint over the stencil with black paint, revealing a crisp, decorative design on the textured, rustic-looking basket, all against a clean white surface.

raw_segments: The video opens with a top-down view of hands, adorned with silver nail polish, meticulously applying gray paint to the rim of a white cylindrical container using a small paintbrush with a teal handle, transforming it into a rustic planter. The workspace is a clean white surface, organized with crafting supplies including a gold-lidded container of gray paint, a small jar of dark brown stain, and a white paper sheet. A female voice narrates, explaining that after the initial steps, she felt "there was still room for improvement," specifically due to "the texture of the plastic" of the container. She mentions toning it down using "Nutmeg by Apple Barrel Paint" for a faux rust effect, dry-brushing it on, and expressing satisfaction with the "great improvement." As she speaks, the scene transitions to show the finished planter, now filled with white pebbles and a vibrant cascade of artificial yellow, pink, and purple flowers, followed by a view of the planter upright with lush green artificial foliage. Upbeat, acoustic guitar-driven instrumental music with a folk/country feel then plays. The video shifts to a new project, featuring a ribbed beige plastic container. The hands are seen holding a package labeled '4 PACK PAQUET DE' and a small terracotta pot. The female voice returns, mentioning she "grabbed this one" (referring to the terracotta piece) for another project and also "grabbed a four-pack of these little mini plant pots" (referring to the beige plastic containers) for a "super cute idea." event_blocks.summary: Hands, adorned with silver nail polish and later wearing transparent plastic gloves, worked on a clean white surface, initially painting a rectangular wooden plank with homemade white chalk paint, intentionally leaving visible paintbrush strokes to mimic faux wood. The same white chalk paint was applied to the exterior of three plastic terracotta pots from the Dollar Tree to mask their cheap appearance. Subsequently, a dark brown antique wax, or stain, was applied to the white-painted plank using a black-handled brush with firm, scratching strokes to create strong faux wood grain lines, with excess wax then wiped off using a crumpled white cloth to enhance the effect. The process was repeated on the white-painted terracotta pots, dabbing the dark brown wax onto their surfaces and rims with the cloth to achieve a mottled, aged, rustic, and weathered look, preserving cute designs on the pots. The speaker noted this technique, using Nutmeg Brown from Apple Barrel Paint and a crumpled white cloth to dab and blend the dark brown stain, was a fantastic method for Dollar Tree pots that one doesn't want to cover, unlike thicker paints that would obscure the design. One pot was rotated during application, and after all three were finished, they were displayed side-by-side, looking like clay while retaining their original designs. Finally, two of the finished pots were placed onto a light-colored wooden plank, referred to as "that little plank," and secured with a hot glue gun, suggesting the creation of a multi-pot planter or decorative arrangement. Throughout the process, a gold-lidded container of white paint, a small jar of dark brown wax, a paintbrush, and a white paper sheet catching spills were present under bright, even lighting.

raw_segments: The video opens with a top-down view of a clean white workspace where a person, whose hands are adorned with silver nail polish, begins painting a rectangular wooden plank. The speaker explains they are applying "one coat of my homemade white chalk paint" to the plank, aiming to create visible "paint brush strokes" to make it "look more like faux wood." Nearby are three terracotta pots and a gold-lidded container of white paint. The person then paints the exterior of one of the terracotta pots with the same white chalk paint, with the speaker clarifying that these are "plastic pots from the Dollar Tree" that "look obviously plastic and they look cheap right off the bat," so painting them with a base coat and brush strokes helps "hide that it's plastic." Afterward, the scene transitions to the person applying a dark brown substance, which the speaker identifies as "antique wax" (though a stain could also be used), over the white-painted plank. Using a black-handled brush, they apply the wax with firm, scratching strokes to intentionally create "nice strong faux wood lines that look like wood grain." The person then uses a crumpled white cloth to wipe off excess wax, enhancing the grain effect. Next, the focus shifts to a white-painted terracotta pot, where the person dabs the dark brown antique wax onto the surface with the cloth, creating a mottled, aged appearance. This process is repeated on the pot’s rim and body, with the person carefully blending the wax to achieve a rustic, weathered look. The speaker concludes by mentioning they are now taking a "paper towel" to continue the process. Throughout, the workspace remains organized, with the gold-lidded container, a small jar of dark brown wax, and a white paper sheet catching spills, all under bright, even lighting.

The video opens with a top-down view of hands, wearing transparent plastic gloves, actively working on three small terracotta pots on a clean white surface. The speaker identifies the material as "Nutmeg Brown from Apple Barrel Paint" and describes the technique, stating, "I'm going to go ahead and tap this on all over," drawing a comparison to faux granite countertop methods. The hands use a crumpled white cloth to dab and blend this dark brown stain over the white-painted pots, creating a mottled, weathered, wood-like effect. The speaker explains this is a "fantastic technique" for "little plant pots from the Dollar Tree" that have "cute designs" one doesn't want to cover, contrasting it with materials like baking soda or plaster of Paris that would create a thick paint and "lose your design." One pot is held and rotated during application, while two others are nearby, partially finished. A gold-lidded container with white paint remnants and a paintbrush indicates an earlier step. The hands then pick up all three pots, displaying them side by side to showcase their uniform, rustic finish, which the speaker notes makes them "look like clay" while preserving the original design. The scene transitions to the placement of two of these pots onto a light-colored wooden plank, which the speaker refers to as "that little plank." event_blocks.summary: Hands with dark nail polish first placed three terracotta pots onto a wooden plank, aligning them side by side, with a female voice explaining the intention to create easily movable plant pots. The pots were then painted white and distressed with a dark brown stain before being filled with artificial succulents, intended as a "super cute summer decor piece" for a mantel. The scene transitioned to a new project involving a metal tray lined with aluminum foil, where a sheet of printed paper featuring a vintage French confectionery logo ('SPECIALITE DE VIOLETTES CANDIES FABRIQUE DE CONFISERIE') was placed. The speaker noted she had prepared the paper by squirting it with watered-down brown paint and letting it dry. Hands applied white adhesive from a foam brush to a light blue metal canister, identified as a "new bucket" from the Dollar Tree, covering one side of the writing with "one coat of my white chalk paint." The printed paper was then aligned and pressed onto the canister, trimmed with pink-handled scissors, and the backing paper meticulously peeled away. Subsequently, the hands peeled small pieces of the printed paper, preparing them for decoupage onto the "so cute" light blue metal canister, using white chalk paint as a base and applying it with a foam brush. The printed paper, featuring the vintage logo and floral illustrations, was aligned and pressed onto the canister, with the speaker explaining she tore the paper to create "nice and ragged" edges before "mod podging it down" to create a "super cute two-sided planter" adaptable to various decor styles. After smoothing air bubbles, a white paint marker was used to trace and fill thin black lines along the design's edges. The canister was then given a distressed, aged finish by applying a dark brown stain with a crumpled cloth, described as using "antiquing wax" for a "nice border." The final scene revealed the transformed canister, now a planter filled with artificial foliage, displayed on a white wooden surface, showcasing the completed DIY project with a French confectionery theme, accompanied by upbeat, folksy background music.

The minute begins with a top-down view of hands, wearing dark nail polish, working on a ribbed, light beige plastic pot placed on a clean white surface. A female narrator explains, "So the next thing I did was take a furniture pen, in black, and I'm going to go ahead and create a really cute border on the top of this plant pot," as the hands use a black furniture repair marker to draw a zigzag pattern along the pot's rim. Gentle, acoustic background music begins to play. After the marker work, the narrator continues, "Once that was dry, I went ahead and started gluing my towering blocks around the top. And I'm using hot glue because a hot glue now will stick to the cord that you glued down. And I'm kind of shoving them up under that rim to lift the rim up just a little bit." During this, the hands apply hot glue with a black and green glue gun to the pot’s edge and attach small rectangular wooden blocks vertically, creating a textured border. The narrator adds, "And once I've done it all the way around the plant pot, I'd go ahead and make a cute little design on the end of the towering blocks. And this is what we end up with," as the hands use the black marker again to draw vertical lines between the blocks. The pot is then shown fully decorated with a black-painted lower half and the wooden-block upper rim, featuring the zigzag pattern at the transition. The background music shifts to an upbeat, electronic instrumental. The final scene reveals the pot transformed into a planter, filled with lush green artificial foliage, displayed against a white background with a soft green gradient border. The video concludes with a black screen featuring the text 'Thanks for watching! XOXO' and a circular logo with 'HUMBLE', accompanied by a prompt to 'Click on my face to'. The female narrator returns, saying, "If you made it all the way to the end of this video, thank you so much. And as always, until the next one, breathe deep, fret not, and do things that make you happy." The music then transitions to a folksy, acoustic guitar melody.

A circular woven logo with 'HUMBLE' in bold black letters appears in the lower right, emitting a faint wisp of smoke. Below the logo, white text prompts viewers to 'Click on my face to subscribe.' After a few seconds, the 'HUMBLE' logo fades, leaving only the subscription text, which is then joined by a small, stylized red heart with 'XOXO' written in white. The screen remains black, emphasizing the call to action and the creator’s affectionate farewell, accompanied by the continuous instrumental guitar melody.
